content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886
September 22, 2022
Kokotaylo Advice Letter No. A-22-070 is SUPERSEDED by Kokotaylo Advice Letter No. A-22-
070(a)
Kristopher J. Kokotaylo
City Attorney, City of Union City
Meyers Nave
1999 Harrison St, 9th Floor
Oakland, Ca 94612
Re: 
Your Request for Advice  
 
Our File No.  A-22-070(a)
Dear Mr. Kokotaylo:
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the 
conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions 
such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Also note that we are not a finder of fact 
when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes 
your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions 
should change, you should contact us for additional advice. 2
QUESTION
Does the Act prohibit Vice Mayor Patiño from taking part in upcoming City Council 
decisions concerning the draft Housing Element given the Inventory includes a property located 
within 500 feet of his residence and another located between 500 and 1,000 feet of his residence?  
CONCLUSION
Yes. As explained below, it is reasonably foreseeable that decisions concerning the draft 
Housing Element will have a material financial effect on his residence.
1  The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18104 through 18998 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.
2  This letter supersedes Kokotaylo Advice Letter, No. A-22-070, dated August 22, 2022. This letter is issued to 
clarify the analysis in regard to the provided facts.


File No. A-22-070(a)
Page No. 2
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER
You are the City Attorney for the City of Union City seeking advice on behalf of Union City 
Vice Mayor Jaime Patiño. He currently owns a home on 9th Street in Union City, which has a 
population of approximately 70,000-75,000 residents and is approximately 19 square miles. 
The City Council will determine whether to provide feedback to staff on the City’s draft 
Housing Element prior to staff sending the draft to the California Department of Housing and 
Community Development (“HCD”) for its initial review and feedback. The City Council will 
ultimately be asked to approve the Housing Element. Any HCD feedback on the draft Housing 
Element will be incorporated into the final Housing Element to be adopted by the City Council.
The Housing Element is one of seven required elements of the City’s General Plan. State 
law (Government Code section 65580 et seq.) specifies in detail the topics that the Housing 
Element must address and sets a schedule for regular updates. State law requires each local 
government to update its Housing Element every eight years. The City is currently in the process of 
updating its Housing Element for the 2023 to 2031 planning period, or sixth cycle.
The Housing Element is also the only element reviewed and certified by the State for 
compliance with State law. As stated above, HCD is the State department responsible for this 
certification. Each jurisdiction in California is required to plan for its fair share of the region’s 
housing need. This fair share is determined through a process called the Regional Housing Need 
Allocation (“RHNA”). HCD identifies the total housing need for each region of the state. Regional 
Councils of Government (e.g., ABAG) are responsible for dividing the regional housing need 
allocation among all of its local cities, towns, and counties. Once a local government has received 
its RHNA, it must revise its Housing Element to show how it plans to accommodate its portion of 
the region’s housing need.
In particular, State law requires that the City “identify sites that can be developed for 
housing within the planning period and that are sufficient to provide for the jurisdiction’s share of 
the regional housing need for all income levels” in the Housing Element. (Government Code 
Section 65583.2(a)) (“Inventory”). Government Code Section 65583(a)(3) specifies that “land 
suitable for residential development” includes vacant sites zoned for residential use. 
In compliance with State law, the City’s draft Inventory identifies thirty-six sites. One of the 
sites identified is a .12-acre site located 158 feet (approximately 1 block) from the Vice Mayor’s 
residence and not likely visible from the property. Another site identified is a .23-acre site located 
921 feet from his residence. Both sites are a vacant lots zoned for residential use permitting one 
single-family home to be constructed on each site. The other thirty-four sites on the Inventory are 
located more than 1,000 feet from the Vice Mayor’s residence. 
With respect to staff feedback, there is no limit on the type of direction the City Council can 
provide. The City Council can simply approve or not approve the submittal of the Housing Element 
to HCD as a whole without any change to the site Inventory, but it is also within the City Council’s 
authority to direct staff to remove properties from the Inventory or add additional properties to the 


File No. A-22-070(a)
Page No. 3
Inventory. The City Council will have similar discretion when it is asked to ultimately approve the 
Housing Element after review by the HCD.3
You stated that staff does not have information concerning the approximate distance 15 
percent of the residential real property in Union City would be from the 36 sites identified. You 
further stated the information is not easily ascertainable.
ANALYSIS
The Act’s conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from taking part in a 
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect on one or more of the official’s financial interests distinguishable from the decision's effect 
on the public generally. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) The financial interests that may give rise to an 
official’s disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act are set forth in Section 87103 including an 
interest in any real property in which the official has an interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 
87103(b).) Therefore, Vice Mayor Patiño has a real property interest in his residence.   
Foreseeability and Materiality
A financial effect on a financial interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the 
financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official 
or the official’s agency. (Regulation 18701(a).) Regarding financial interests not explicitly involved 
in a decision, as here, a financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. 
In general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than 
hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. If the financial result cannot be expected 
absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official’s control, it is not reasonably 
foreseeable. (Regulation 18701(b).)
Regulation 18702.2 provides materiality standards for determining when a reasonably 
foreseeable effect on an interest in real property is material. Applicable to the .12-acre site on the 
draft Inventory located 158 feet from the Vice Mayor’s residence, Regulation 18702.2(a)(7) 
provides that the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on an official’s 
real property parcel is material whenever the decision involves property located 500 feet or less 
from the property line of the parcel, unless there is clear and convincing evidence that the decision 
will not have any measurable impact on the official’s property. 
Your facts state the property site is a vacant lot zoned for residential use to permit one 
single-family home to be constructed on it. While being listed on the inventory does not necessarily 
mean the lot will be used for housing for any particular income level, whether the lot is included or 
removed from the inventory may potentially implicate the future use of the property and therefore 
the value of the property and those properties within 500 feet of the property. For instance, 
including a property on the inventory may discourage future use of the property for any other 
purposes if only to ensure that the City is not required to find additional properties to satisfy its 
3 The sites are currently located in a single-family, residentially zoned district and are not proposed to be 
rezoned in the draft Housing Element. 


File No. A-22-070(a)
Page No. 4
housing needs. Conversely, removing a property from the list could increase the likelihood that the 
property will be available or used in the future for purposes other than housing. In either 
circumstance, a property’s inclusion or removal from the inventory could implicate the future use of 
the property in a manner which may have measurable impacts on the property and those properties 
within 500 feet or the property, especially considering the purpose of the inventory is to ensure the 
City has identified and designated sufficient properties to be able to meet its future housing needs 
for all income levels. Therefore, there is not clear and convincing evidence that decisions 
concerning whether to keep the lot 158 feet from the Vice Mayor’s residence on, or remove it from, 
the draft Inventory will not have any measurable impact on his property. 
In addition, there is a vacant .23-acre site on the draft Inventory located 921 feet from the 
Vice Mayor’s residence and multiple sites 1,000 feet or more from the residence. Regulation 
18702.2(a)(8) provides the applicable materiality standard for real property within 500 to 1,000 feet 
from the property involved in a decision. Under Regulation 18702.2(a)(8), a decision’s effect on an 
official’s real property interest is material if the decision would change the parcel’s market value, 
development potential, income producing potential, highest and best use, or character by 
substantially altering traffic levels, intensity of use, parking, view, privacy, noise levels, or air 
quality. For the other sites, Regulation 18702.2(b) provides that the financial effect of a decision 
involving property 1,000 feet or more from the property line of the official’s property is presumed 
not to be material, and this presumption may be rebutted with clear and convincing evidence the 
governmental decision would have a substantial effect on the official’s property. 
As we have determined that the Vice Mayor is disqualified from the decisions due to the 
foreseeable financial effect the decision will have on his residence 158 feet from one of the sites on 
the draft inventory, we do not further analyze the potential effect on his residence should the lot 158 
feet from the residence be removed from the list or the decisions be segmented as detailed below. 
However, we note that facts provided do not indicate that separate decisions concerning whether the 
vacant lot more than 900 feet away remains on the draft Inventory or is removed from it will change 
the value of, or have any of the other impacts on, the Vice Mayor’s residence. There is also no 
indication that decisions regarding the lots more than 1,000 feet from the residence would have a 
substantial effect on the official’s property. To the extent that the lot 158 feet from the residence is 
first removed from the list or that decisions regarding the lot are segmented from the other 
decisions, the Vice Mayor may wish to seek further advice. 
Public Generally Exception
Commonly referred to as the “public generally” exception, Regulation 18703(a) permits a 
public official to take part in a governmental decision that affects one or more of the official’s 
interests if the decision’s financial effect on the interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the 
public generally. (See Section 87103.) In general, an effect on an official’s interest is 
indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if a significant segment of the public is 
affected and the effect on the official’s interest is not unique when compared to the effect on the 
significant segment of the public. (Regulation 18703(a).) Applicable to these facts, where the only 
interest an official has in the decision is the official's primary residence, Regulation 
18703(b)(1)(C)(2) defines a “significant segment” as at least 15 percent of residential real property 
within the official’s jurisdiction. A unique effect is defined to include a disproportionate effect on 
an interest in real property resulting from the proximity of a project. (Regulation 18703(c).)


File No. A-22-070(a)
Page No. 5
Here, because you stated that staff does not have information concerning the approximate 
distance 15 percent of the residential real property in Union City is from the 36 sites identified, and 
that such information is not easily ascertainable, we do not further address this issue.4
Segmentation
We note that in some instances decisions can be segmented to allow for the participation of 
the official. Generally, under Regulation 18706 an agency may segment a decision in which a 
public official has a financial interest, to allow participation by the official in other, later decisions, 
provided the decision can be broken down into separate decisions that are not “inextricably 
interrelated,” the decision creating the conflict is decided first and finally, and the official's 
participation in later, segmented decisions, does not result in a reopening of the decision in which 
the official has a financial interest. For example, because there are 36 sites for consideration 
regarding the Inventory, it may be possible to segment this particular decision to allow the Vice 
Mayor to participate. Please seek additional advice, as needed, if you have questions regarding the 
segmentation of particular decisions.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Dave Bainbridge  
 
General Counsel
By: 
Jack Woodside                                           
Jack Woodside                                            
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
JW:aja
4 Should this information become available, you may wish to seek additional advice concerning the 
applicability of the public generally exception.

sections.question:
Does the Act prohibit Vice Mayor Patiño from taking part in upcoming City Council 
decisions concerning the draft Housing Element given the Inventory includes a property located 
within 500 feet of his residence and another located between 500 and 1,000 feet of his residence?
sections.conclusion:
Yes. As explained below, it is reasonably foreseeable that decisions concerning the draft 
Housing Element will have a material financial effect on his residence.2  This letter supersedes Kokotaylo Advice Letter, No. A-22-070, dated August 22, 2022. This letter is issued to 
clarify the analysis in regard to the provided facts.


(a)

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does the Act prohibit Vice Mayor Patiño from taking part in upcoming City Council 
decisions concerning the draft Housing Element given the Inventory includes a property located 
within 500 feet of his residence and another located between 500 and 1,000 feet of his residence?

CONCLUSION: Yes. As explained below, it is reasonably foreseeable that decisions concerning the draft 
Housing Element will have a material financial effect on his residence.2  This letter supersedes Kokotaylo Advice Letter, No. A-22-070, dated August 22, 2022. This letter is issued to 
clarify the analysis in regard to the provided facts.


(a)
